output.summary: The CMS Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program was established by the Affordable Care Act and began penalizing hospitals in fiscal year 2013. It applies to general short-term acute care hospitals under Medicare's Inpatient Prospective Payment System and tracks six conditions including heart failure pneumonia and acute myocardial infarction. Penalties of up to 3% of total Medicare base operating payments are assessed using an Excess Readmission Ratio comparing a hospital's readmission rate to an expected benchmark. For fiscal year 2026 approximately 78% of eligible hospitals face some penalty with about 240 hospitals losing at least 1% of Medicare payments.
preserved_facts: Program established by the Affordable Care Act; penalties began in fiscal year 2013
Applies to general short-term acute care hospitals under Medicare Inpatient Prospective Payment System
Six tracked conditions: acute myocardial infarction COPD heart failure pneumonia CABG surgery and elective total hip or knee replacement
Maximum penalty is 3% of total Medicare base operating payments
Penalty metric is the Excess Readmission Ratio (ERR); hospitals above 1.0 are penalized
FY 2026: approximately 78% of eligible hospitals face some penalty; about 240 hospitals lose at least 1%
FY 2026 performance period covers July 1 2021 through June 30 2024 (three-year window)
Starting FY 2027 CMS shortens the measurement window to two years
Program includes risk adjustment and peer group stratification; planned readmissions are excluded
